title: Visa Pay
tagline: Virtual cards, wallets, and Scan-to-Pay on iOS for Visa's Pay-By-Wallet platform.
overview: An iOS application built for Visa's Pay-By-Wallet (PBW) platform — provisioning virtual cards on demand, managing wallet contents, and paying merchants via Scan-to-Pay. The app sits on top of Juspay's payments infrastructure and integrates a wide REST API surface across card lifecycle, authentication, and settlement.
overviewExtra: I owned the iOS feature set end-to-end — from API integration and state design to a small SwiftUI design system that's now used across new Visa features beyond PBW.
problem: Visa needed a polished iOS surface for PBW. The challenge wasn't the feature list — it was making multi-step flows feel inevitable: interruption-safe, error-honest, and quick on slow networks.
approach: I treated each flow as an explicit finite state machine and let SwiftUI render off the state, never the reverse. Network errors, backgrounding, and low-signal conditions all turn into legible UI states instead of crashes or frozen screens. Reusable primitives (buttons, sheets, form rows) compose into screens so feature dev moves at design speed.

techChoices.reason: Native composition beats UIKit bridges for design-led product work — primitives moved fast and stayed accessible.
Reactive state pipelines without leaking subscriptions across views.
Fewer dependencies, full control over retry and error semantics in a payments context.

keyFeatures.title: Virtual card provisioning
keyFeatures.description: On-demand card generation with secure tokenization and full lifecycle management — issue, freeze, replace.

keyFeatures.title: Wallet creation & management
keyFeatures.description: Create, fund, and manage digital wallets with balance tracking, transaction history, and lifecycle events.

keyFeatures.title: Scan-to-Pay flow
keyFeatures.description: QR capture with merchant verification, malformed-payload handling, and a recoverable error surface for every failure mode.

keyFeatures.title: SwiftUI design system
keyFeatures.description: A small set of buttons, sheets, and form rows. New screens compose from primitives — feature dev time dropped noticeably.

challenges.problem: Multi-step flows had to remain interruption-safe — backgrounding the app or losing connectivity mid-flow couldn't corrupt state.
challenges.solution: Every state transition persists. The UI reconstructs the correct step on relaunch — turning a network blip from a bug report into a no-op.
challenges.learning: Modeling flows as explicit state machines pays for itself the first time a user comes back from a backgrounded app and everything just works.

challenges.problem: Scan-to-Pay had to handle camera permissions, malformed QR payloads, and merchant-not-found responses without dropping users into a dead end.
challenges.solution: A single error surface maps every failure (system, parsing, network, business) to a recoverable UI state with a clear next action — never a silent fail.
challenges.learning: In payments, an honest error message is worth more than a thousand happy-path animations. Users forgive failure; they don't forgive silence.

challenges.problem: UI components were duplicated across screens early in the project, slowing iteration.
challenges.solution: Refactored into a small SwiftUI design system. New screens compose from primitives.
challenges.learning: Pull the component library out of the second screen, not the tenth — the cost of refactor grows non-linearly.

outcome: Three core flows shipped to production: virtual card generation, wallet creation, and Scan-to-Pay. The internal SwiftUI primitives are now used across new features beyond PBW — turning a one-off project into shared platform value.

title: MFA & CNP SDK
tagline: A type-safe, cross-platform SDK powering Card-Not-Present payments and Multi-Factor Auth on iOS + Android.
overview: A shared business-logic layer written in PureScript that compiles to JavaScript and runs inside both iOS and Android wrappers. It encodes the complete state machine for CNP transactions and MFA challenges — meaning both platforms share one source of truth for behavior, while each retains a fully native UI.
overviewExtra: The goal: make platform parity a build-time guarantee rather than a release-checklist item. PRs fixing CNP or MFA edge cases now ship to both platforms simultaneously.
problem: Two platforms had drifted: a CNP fix on iOS could go un-shipped on Android for weeks. Every edge case in the auth and payment flow was being implemented twice — and the second copy was always slightly behind.
approach: Modeled the full state space — every transaction status, every MFA challenge — as PureScript ADTs. The compiler refuses to ship a build that forgets a case. Native UI on each platform; one shared brain. The boundary between PureScript and the host runtime is a single explicitly-typed envelope; everything past it stays inside the type system.

techChoices.reason: Row polymorphism + algebraic data types catch entire categories of bugs at compile time.
Runs in JavaScriptCore on iOS and V8 on Android — small, predictable runtime, no platform-specific binaries.
Serialization happens at one boundary; everything past it stays inside the type system.

keyFeatures.title: Typed state machine
keyFeatures.description: Every CNP transaction and MFA challenge state is an ADT case. Forgetting a state is a compile error, not a production incident.

keyFeatures.title: Native UI on each platform
keyFeatures.description: iOS keeps its Swift/SwiftUI surface. Android keeps its Kotlin surface. The shared SDK is the brain, not the body.

keyFeatures.title: FFI envelope boundary
keyFeatures.description: A single serialization layer between PureScript and the host runtime — the only place 'untyped' lives.

keyFeatures.title: Versioned SDK artifact
keyFeatures.description: Both apps consume the SDK as a versioned package. A behavioral fix is one PR, two app updates — no coordination tax.

challenges.problem: Bridging PureScript to native runtimes risked runtime errors that defeat the purpose of static typing.
challenges.solution: Designed a minimal, explicitly typed envelope at each boundary. The 'untyped' part is a single, audited surface — not scattered across the codebase.
challenges.learning: Type safety is only as strong as your weakest boundary. Make that boundary explicit, narrow, and well-tested.

challenges.problem: Onboarding new contributors to PureScript inside an OOP-heavy team.
challenges.solution: Wrote internal docs and worked examples mapping idiomatic OOP patterns to FP equivalents. Lowered the ramp-up barrier without diluting the code.
challenges.learning: FP isn't 'better' than OOP in the abstract — it just makes different things easier to reason about. Teaching the *correspondence* helps people port intuition over.

challenges.problem: Synchronizing releases across two app teams used to require ceremony.
challenges.solution: The shared SDK ships as a versioned artifact both apps consume. A behavioral fix is one PR, two app updates — no coordination tax.
challenges.learning: Coordination cost is a tax on velocity. Architecture that removes it pays dividends every sprint, not just the first one.

outcome: Behavioral parity between iOS and Android became a build-time guarantee, not a release-checklist item. PRs fixing CNP edge cases now land on both platforms simultaneously — the coordination tax dropped to near zero.

title: Food Delivery App
tagline: A 6+ page full-stack ordering app — auth, cart, search, orders. Built solo to learn the stack end-to-end.
overview: An end-to-end food ordering web application built solo to learn the full stack without hiding behind a framework. Server-rendered pages with vanilla JS for interactivity, MongoDB for persistence, secure user authentication, a persistent cart, search across menu items, order placement, and a UI that holds up on phones.
overviewExtra: I picked an app domain I already understood (food ordering) and committed to building it solo, top to bottom — to develop a complete mental model of how a request travels from form to database and back.
problem: I wanted to learn the whole stack end-to-end without hiding behind a framework. I needed to build the muscle memory for HTTP, sessions, persistence, and auth — not just the abstractions on top.
approach: Server-rendered pages, vanilla JS for interactivity, MongoDB for persistence. Cart state lives server-side keyed to the session — refreshes don't drop it. Auth uses bcrypt with a sensible work factor. Shared layout partials and a small CSS utility set keep the 6+ pages cohesive without a framework.

techChoices.reason: Smallest viable backend — learn fundamentals, not a framework.
Schema-on-read fit the iterative shape of a learning project.
Built the habit early — never store plaintext, even in toy projects.

keyFeatures.title: Secure authentication
keyFeatures.description: Bcrypt-hashed passwords with a sensible work factor. Session-based login, no plaintext stored or logged.

keyFeatures.title: Server-side cart
keyFeatures.description: Cart state lives on the server keyed to the session. Refreshes and re-logins don't lose state — simpler than client-side hydration tricks.

keyFeatures.title: Search across menu
keyFeatures.description: Live search across menu items with debounced lookup and graceful empty states.

keyFeatures.title: Responsive UI
keyFeatures.description: Single layout that holds up across phones, tablets, and desktops without a framework.

challenges.problem: Maintaining cart state across pages without a frontend framework.
challenges.solution: Persisted the cart server-side keyed to the session. Refreshes and re-logins don't lose state.
challenges.learning: State doesn't have to live on the client. For request-driven apps, server-side state is often simpler and more reliable.

challenges.problem: Storing passwords safely in a learning project where it would have been easy to skip.
challenges.solution: Used bcrypt with a sensible work factor and never stored or logged plaintext.
challenges.learning: Security habits formed in toy projects carry over. Skipping them once makes the next compromise easier.

challenges.problem: Making a 6+ page site feel cohesive without a design system.
challenges.solution: Extracted shared partials (header, footer, nav) and a small CSS utility set — a poor man's component library.
challenges.learning: The principles behind component libraries are independent of any framework. You can practice them in vanilla, just less ergonomically.

outcome: A 6+ page full-stack application with auth, cart, search, and orders shipped end-to-end — and, more importantly, a complete mental model of how a request travels from form to database and back. That intuition is still paying off two years later.
